meta.title: Demonstrate understanding of mechanical systems
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves demonstrating understanding of mechanical systems.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Provides accurate definitions of core physical quantities such as force, torque, or angular velocity.

positive_signals: Provides accurate definitions using correct terminology
Includes directionality for vector quantities
Uses specific examples to illustrate definitions
negative_signals: Omits key aspects like direction or units
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Definitions must be precise and include directional information for vector quantities like force.

success_examples: centripetal force is directed toward the centre of the circle

omis.description: Draws a force diagram with all relevant forces in correct directions and locations.

positive_signals: Includes all significant forces acting on the object
Labels forces with correct names
Shows forces in correct directions and locations
negative_signals: Missing one or more significant forces
evidence_guide.common_confusion: All relevant forces must be represented in the diagram, not just the most obvious ones.

omis.description: Correctly uses angular kinematic relationships to solve for unknowns.

positive_signals: Uses correct equations like ω = θ/t or θ = ωt
Performs proper substitution of values
Calculates unknowns with correct units
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Angular kinematics equations must be applied with correct relationships and units.

success_examples: calculated angular velocity using rotational kinematics

omis.description: Identifies that net force arises from the sum of multiple interacting forces.

positive_signals: States net force as the vector sum of forces
Describes the direction of the net force
Links net force to unbalanced forces
negative_signals: Lists forces without calculating the sum
Ignores the direction of net force
Treats net force as a single independent force
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Net force is the resultant vector sum of all forces, not just any individual force.

success_examples: recognised that the net force provided the centripetal force

omis.description: Applies conservation of energy reasoning across multiple energy forms.

positive_signals: States initial and final energy forms
Equates total energy with reasoning
Links transformations between energy forms
negative_signals: Uses a single formula without linkage
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Conservation requires equating total energy across all forms, not just one type.

success_examples: solved problems using the conservation of energy with mistake(s)

omis.description: Explains how mass distribution affects rotational inertia or acceleration.

positive_signals: References formulas like I ∝ mr²
Links mass layout to changes in inertia
Explains effects on rotational motion
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Mass distribution affects rotational inertia, not just the total mass of the object.

success_examples: explain why rotational inertia decreases using a valid formulae or relationship

omis.description: Identifies how energy is lost from a system and the physical mechanism responsible.

positive_signals: Links energy loss to specific form (e.g., heat, sound)
Identifies the physical cause (e.g., friction)
Explains the mechanism of loss
negative_signals: General references to 'energy loss' without specifics
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Energy loss must be tied to a specific physical process like friction or air resistance.

success_examples: energy is lost as heat due to friction during damping

omis.description: Compares relative force magnitudes or directions across different system conditions.

positive_signals: Describes how net forces vary between conditions
Includes direction and cause of variations
Provides comparative analysis
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comparison requires analyzing differences in force conditions between system states.

success_examples: comprehensive discussion of the net force
relative net forces for different positions

omis.description: Uses physics equations and working to logically justify conclusions.

positive_signals: Follows logical progression: known → formula → substitution → result → conclusion
Uses correct equations and substitutions
Provides clear step-by-step working
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Justification requires showing the complete reasoning process, not just the final answer.

success_examples: produced clear logical and sequential layout

omis.description: Analyses a system's energy transformation sequence and explains cause–effect impact.

positive_signals: Traces the sequence of energy transformations
Explains cause–effect relationships
Links transformations to system changes
negative_signals: No explanation of impacts on motion
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Analysis must follow the chain of transformations and their resulting effects on the system.

success_examples: reference to all energy transformations and cause and impact of loss
